reviews.review_text: Good game, worth your time on sale, but you won't be able to take my word for it when the launcher doesn't ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ work. GGs Ubislop

When Watch_Dogs launched in 2014, it was unfairly crushed under the weight of massive hype and its infamous E3 graphical downgrade. Looking back today, its dark, rain-slicked version of Chicago offers a gritty techno-thriller atmosphere that the colorful sequels completely lost. Playing as the brooding vigilante Aiden Pearce feels like stepping into a modern John Wick movie where your smartphone is just as lethal as your grenade launcher. Triggering steam pipes to blow up enemy cars, manipulating traffic lights mid-chase, and infiltrating gang hideouts through security cameras makes the tactical gameplay incredibly satisfying. If you grab it cheap on a Steam sale, dealing with the annoying Ubisoft launcher is a tiny price to pay for such a gem.

Writing this review right after completing the last achievement, Disk Space Full, and honestly, at one point I wanted to give up on getting 100% completion just because I couldn’t find any new songs from pedestrians(Which is required to get Disk Space Full achievement). But I’m glad I didn’t.
This game is everything that Ubisoft no longer seems to be: creative, ambitious, and surprisingly deep.
That said, I do have to mention that the multiplayer mode doesn’t work properly on the Steam version. The issue can be fixed fairly easily through support, but it really shouldn’t exist in the first place.
As for the story, Aiden Pearce is a very well-written character. I wouldn’t say he’s perfectly written, and the story itself doesn’t always make perfect sense, but it’s more than enough to make me empathize with him.

We all remember that E3 trailer. We all felt the betrayal when the final game got hit with the nerf bat harder than anything in gaming history. But here's the thing, once you actually play it, Watch Dogs is a pretty decent time. Hacking traffic lights, blowing up steam pipes, snooping through random NPCs' phones to find out Dave from accounting is having a rough week, genuinely fun stuff. Aiden Pearce himself has the personality of a damp napkin and the story is mid at best, but the open world holds up well enough to carry it. Not the revolution it promised, but nowhere near the disaster the internet made it out to be.
[b][7.5/10][/b]

This game had a bad rep in 2014, but slowly with updates and time it is definitely a classic game + the T-Bone DLC was good touch from devs.

Time to replay WD2

9/10 ( -1 point for the atroucious aim-assist, u cant turn it off, its buggy someetimes..)
Amazing game with an equally amazing story. Side content can be mediocre but most is entertaining, not a single watching dog tho so its not perfect.

[h1]Watch Dogs[/h1]
Watch Dogs is an open world third-person action-adventure game that has elements of both a RPG and a Shooter with very cool hacking abilities.  I pre-ordered it as soon as it was available, and it did not disappoint. Absolutely amazing game for its time!  

[h1]Port Status[/h1]
Watch Dogs plays great. The controls work well with the exception or driving physics, which make controlling vehicles awkward on mouse and keyboard, making me switch between my controller and M/K when I was behind the wheel. 

[h1]Graphics[/h1]
Ubisoft hit the nail on the head bringing Chicago alive and optimizing this game.  The graphics were Next Gen for their time and still look great.

[h1]Story[/h1]
I really enjoyed the story and the hacking features were something new to me and really fit well with the story/ gameplay style. The environment is easy to immerse yourself in and seemed alive. One of the best features in this game is the music while driving, fantastic sound tracks. Aiden Pierce is one of the best protagonists ever, making the story very engaging. The DLCs are great too. 

[h1]Bugs[/h1]
The game had bugs at launch but most of them were patched out. Online was a flop with no anti-cheat, not to mention connectivity issues. After 105 hrs in this game, I still enjoy running around in game, driving, hacking, and interacting with the environment.  

[h1]Conculsion[/h1]
The game is a must play and I highly recommend it. Two thumbs up!

First of all, an underrated game that still holds up incredibly well. Watch Dogs delivers a darker and more grounded atmosphere compared to many modern open-world games. The hacking mechanics feel creative and satisfying, the Chicago setting is immersive, and Aiden Pearce is a surprisingly compelling protagonist once you dive into the story.

In addition, the soundtrack, rainy visuals, and tense vigilante vibe give the game a unique identity that separates it from other Ubisoft titles. Even years later, the gameplay still feels fun and fresh. Definitely worth playing if ya enjoy story-driven open-world games with a serious tone!

Ok, so I have had this game for some time and came back for a revisit. I generally enjoy the game. there are a few philosophical problems with the game however. You wither play all in mr good guy or mostly mr good guy who does only mildy care about the citizens. I would prefer a full rougue option, let the damn world burn. Without giving spoilers its a good perspective that is needed to this story. Alas, that is not our path. A self righteous, emotionally unaware "hacker". Maybe if they gave our star a ... form or level of tism it might make sense. We trudge forward single minded like a 16 yr old savage with innate skill and no awareness of self.

All that said, its an interesting game. For those that like hacking culture from afar with out the knowledge or skills of the real. Recommend as I'm just a bastard picky about my games.

[h1]Rating: 7/10 -Decent[/h1]

[h1]TLDR:[/h1]
[b]This game is half coal, half diamond. A memorable experience with tons of flaws but with a great vibe.[/b]


Having played through almost all of the AC and Farcry games, I turned my attention to a series I ignored for many years. Watch_Dogs just seemed like a GTA clone from Ubisoft with hacking. That sentence is quite insulting after playing the game since it has unique qualities, ideas and a vibe that I don't find anywhere else. I knew the game was about mass surveilangce and internet freedom and I thought to myself, now is the time to play this. It's no secret that the government watches our every step and privacy is something that gets scarcer year by year. The online age verification being pushed down people's throats is a recent example. They tell you, it's for safety, that they won't save the image but that is just a straight up lie and your data will end up stolen thanks to an idiotic IT department. Watch_Dogs is as close as we can get to a modern day AC game.


[h1]Positive:[/h1]

[b]Story:[/b]
Aiden Pearce and his buddy hacked a hotel to siphon money. Aiden gets detected but manages to flee. A hitman gets sent to Aiden. He causes a car crash which Aiden survives, but his niece called Lena, doesn't. He's now out for revenge and tries to track down the person that ordered the hit. The search starts out pretty simple but the deeper you dig, the more you uncover the hidden crime and organisations of Chicago. The story didn't grab me instantly but the further I got into it, the more interesting it got. I'm the type of guy that likes to play side content but the story was so interesting that I straight up ignored most of the side content and focused entirely on playing the story. Not every story mission is a banger but there were a handful that were memorable and had a great vibe. 

[b]World:[/b]
The game takes place in Chicago. The world is decently designed. The game starts out in the suburbs but you will later enter the city. The city is mostly grey concrete but there is an area with more nature. The world didn't look ground breaking but I liked it for what it is.

[b]Restaurants:[/b]
You can enter restaurants and coffee shops to drink something. Drinking coffee or soda will increase your focus meter temporarily. The focus meter lets you slow down time in gun fights. I really like how this is kind of a role play mechanic that rewards you for interacting. It increases immersion and makes me feel like I am a part of this world.

[b]Aiden Pearce:[/b]
I‘ve heard some people say that Aiden is a weak character. I have to disagree. He's kind of a broken person. He feels guilty for the death of his niece and he's not able to let go. He has the need to make up for it. He becomes a vigilante. He uses ctOS's crime prediction algorithm to detect possible crime. He helps random people. He takes justice into his own hands. He ain't a saint though. He steals cars and kills people without due process. I think, this is kind of based. People don't act. They watch and film incidents but they won't do anything about it. We need more people like Aiden in this world that don't take sh*t from assh*les. The only type of clothing he wears are trench coats. They look awesome and move in the wind (the cloth physics are a bit messed up though). Aiden is a silent protector, hidden in the shadows. He is kind of a modern day Ezio.

[b]Movement Animations:[/b]
Aiden looks like he has weight and needs effort to move quickly (he’s not fat). I don’t know how to describe it but the characters move like real humans. Going in and out of sprint isn’t instant. Vaulting and climbing over things looks great too, thanks to parkour inspired animations. I liked the fluid animation of GTA 5 but I think that WD1 did it even better and added some style.

[b]Takedowns:[/b]
Like in every Ubisoft game, you can do takedowns. If you’re in stealth, Aiden will use his baton to punch enemies. If you’re are in a gunfight and you come close to an enemy with your gun, you can take him down with your weapon at point blank range. Those takedowns look incredibly cold and efficient. There is one animation where Aiden kicks an enemy to ground and shoots him in the back of the head. Absolute aura. Gun takedowns are always a treat in combat. 

[b]Vibe:[/b]
I love the vibe of the game. It’s dark and distopian. The colors are muted. The city ist mostly grey concrete. Aiden is dead set on getting revenge. You're walking through the streets with a trench coat. You are hidden in plain sight, scanning everyone's private life. Aiden has immense amounts of aura.

[b]Digital Trips:[/b]
Digital trips are side content that the game never mentions. They're fun little mini games. One lets you control a giant robotic spider and another one let's you drive a car that's on fire through masses of zombies. My favorite one was called "Alone". The whole city is empty and it's darkened by weird generators. You have to sneak past robots from skibidi toilet to get to the generator (the robots have cameras as heads). All of those mini games are really weird and have nothing to do with the main game. I really liked them. They were a surreal and refreshing experience.


[h1]Negative:[/h1]

[b]Audio:[/b]
I've been making music for five years and I have a decent headphone audio setup that is perfectly configured for gaming and music creation. Watch Dogs 1 has caused some trouble. It is too quiet. Every audio setting is maxed out but the game is still too quiet. I've searched for a solution on YouTube, Reddit and I've even asked ChatGPT. I found a couple of people that had the same low audio issues. They all recommended to change some windows settings but they were already perfectly configured. I've been using this audio setup since 2023 and I've probably played over 60 games in that time span without any audio issues (even older games like HL2, Portal and gta 4 worked perfectly). My audio interface has a knob that I could turn up more but I don't want to do this. My audio settings are perfect and I don't want to change them up for some broke ass 12 year old game. I gave up and continued to play the game with low volume.

[b]Hackable Objects:[/b]
There are surprisingly few things to hack in a game that is all about hacking. You can hack the phones of people, cameras, traffic lights and power transformers and other things. It just felt too little. It wasn't a big problem to me though. The other gameplay elements were great.

[b]Side Missions:[/b]
They are fine but way too repetetive and you need to do alot if you want to unlock the achievements.

[b]High FPS Issues:[/b]
I originally intended to criticise the recoil of guns and the movement since both felt terrible. I later found out on my own that those problems can be fixed by locking the game to 60 FPS. I've got a 300hz monitor and a powerful PC that runs the game at 230-280 FPS. This causes the recoil to just twitch around like crazy, feeling very unatural and bad. movement looks extremely broken when sprinting and pressing A and D rapidly. It looks like you teleport around. all of this can be fixed by just locking the game to 60 FPS. The issue is, I don't want to play games in 60 FPS.

[b]Online:[/b]
Online components of the game do not work in the Steam version. They do work in the Ubisoft connect version. I was sadly unable to experience the multiplayer. 

[b]One Save Slot:[/b]
Like every Ubisoft game from 2014, the game has only one save slot. If I want to replay this gem, I need to back up my save and delete the save from the game to play it again.


[h1]Conclusion:[/h1]

This game was left in a bad shape by ubi. GTA 4's PC port is even worse though. There are some mods out there that are able to fix the game. I really liked Watch_Dogs 1 and I was extremely surprised by it. I recommend giving it a try on sale.

phenominal game. i grew up watching charles bronson in death wish so a little vigilante sim where you shoot badguys ♥♥♥♥♥♥ rocks. the story is slowburn but its got depth in its themes on the act of killing, surveillance, and the effects your actions have on your family. its got dozens of side activities that are fun but i personally never did the fixer contracts. you can just brute force through the action with crime intervention and gang hideouts and criminal convoys, so levelling up your skill tree is actually interesting and fun, kind of an issue in other ubisoft titles. if youre a loner male with fixations on justice this game is definitely for you. its gritty, psuedo-noir and im very fond of it.

Watch Dogs is good.

That's my hot take about it. I don't even care if it was that era of Ubisoft.. I Finished it on 360, Finished on the Xbox One, came back to it years later on PC.

Now.. for Ultrawide users such as myself. GOOD GOD THE FOV IS UNBEARABLE. And that was my problem for years. No matter how many fixes I tried. Nothing.. that was until NexusTools came out and that problem went away. Other than some AMD Driver timeout crashes I had a few times from me not capping the framerate. The game worked fine. Threw in Living City and The Fall of The Winding City and had a blast. I haven't finished Bad Blood at all but maybe this time I will.. so if I had to recommend this game.. Get it on sale. If you want a little bit extra Get The Legion Ultimate Edition on discount since that bundle includes this game. Then install Nexustools and raise the FOV then install Living City. Overall.. it's still good years later. Worth the replays I did do..

Watch_Dogs is easily one of the best games in the whole series. I’ve played all of them and this first one still stands out. Watch_Dogs 2 was also solid, but Legion felt like sloppy, lazy work. This one though? Awesome sauce.
The story is genuinely great — hacking into Chicago’s ctOS system, the revenge plot, the atmosphere… it all sucked me in. The gameplay loop of hacking traffic lights, cameras, phones, and causing chaos is super satisfying. I ended up putting in 9 hours and could’ve easily gone longer. The world feels alive and the whole “they’re always watching us through our phones” vibe hits different.
If you like open-world games with a tech-hacking twist and a decent story, this is still a really fun experience even years later. Definitely my favorite Watch_Dogs game.

Watch Dogs is still one of those open world games that deserves more credit than it got.

Chicago looks great, the atmosphere is gritty and stylish and the whole idea of turning your phone into a hacker's dream weapon makes the world feel way more dangerous and fun than your usual open-world checklist.

Aiden works well as a colder more serious lead and the story keeps enough tension to carry the whole game without falling apart.

The hacking is the real hook here, blowing up explosives, messing with cameras, controlling traffic lights, or setting up clean takedowns never really gets old. The game does have weak spots though: some missions repeat a bit too much, driving feels clunky at times, and the amount of actual hacking is not always as deep as the premise makes you hope. 

The online side also feels like a missed opportunity now, especially with cheaters around and Ubisoft no longer really supporting it.

Still for all its flaws this is a strong and memorable game. It has style, a solid soundtrack, good mission variety and enough personality to stand out even years later.

I remember wanting to play this game so badly back in the day. Well, I did. 

It's okay. 

I think it might have been pretty good for its time. But it's also frustrating. The world is ok, a bit boring, but that's fine. It's pretty and got lots of dark alleys and stuff, which I like. A city rat simulator, exactly the kind of stuff I like.

However, there are just so many frustrations. Not even with the story, but the side missions are bland, driving is pretty awful, chases range from extremely simple to impossible (only they don't range, there's just two settings). 

You have to find your own fun, and that's not a great approach to such a game, I think.

Shooting is okay, hacking is fine. But that's the deal. There's nothing great about this game. It's just fine.

Also I hated all the little hitches this game has. Like you often get caught on a rock or something else you didn't see. Climable objects turn out to be not so climable, etc. Frustrating, but atmospheric

Story is also kinda disappointing, to be honest. There are so many threads that just get cut off or not developed fully. 

In general, a great world and a meh game.
I love this game and all the other entries in the series. You just don't get this amount of creativity in how you approach fights anymore.

The first time I played Watch Dogs was on the Xbox One in 2018 and I loved the game because it was dark and serious and it was fun controlling the world of Chicago and causing chaos on the street. I enjoyed the Batman aura of Aiden Pearce as he hunts the men responsible for what happened, I love playing the game as a ninja and using stealth to get around the map but stealth isn't always possible with some missions requiring you to use brute force to get what you want. I enjoyed the hacking minigames and completing the puzzles around the map, I liked playing the online activities like hacking other players and stealing their data but it seems that in 2025 and 2026 the servers are offline making some of the achievements impossible to get. When the game first released in 2014 a lot of players were upset about the quality of the game but I loved the game in 2018 and the fact that Ubisoft predicted the future with major tech companies owning every media and other stuff that doesn't need to be mentioned. I recommend this game to anyone who enjoys games like GTA, Batman, Cyberpunk and Deus Ex: Human Revolution which is a game I want to play.

A gritty, iconic origin story that walked so the rest of the franchise could run.

Watch_Dogs is the classic that successfully laid the groundwork for a massive franchise, and in many ways, its narrative remains the absolute peak of the series. Unlike the colorful, lighthearted, hipster-hacker vibe of its sequels, the story of Aiden Pearce is a dark, grounded, and genuinely compelling tale of a vigilante out for revenge. Running around a rainy, atmospheric Chicago in an iconic trench coat feels incredibly cinematic, and the game does a fantastic job of pulling you into its tech-noir criminal underworld. It takes itself very seriously, and that gritty tone gives the campaign a heavy, satisfying weight that keeps you hooked from start to finish.

However, to enjoy that story, you have to come to terms with the massive, four-wheeled elephant in the room: the driving physics. Calling the vehicle handling "bad" is almost an understatement; it is downright weird. Every single car you steal feels like it weighs 10,000 pounds while simultaneously driving on a street made entirely of butter. Taking a sharp corner at high speeds usually results in you either miraculously drifting like a pro or violently wrapping a sedan around a light pole, with absolutely zero middle ground. It is a deeply jarring mechanical quirk that you simply have to endure if you want to survive the intense police chases.

What truly saves the gameplay and defines the game’s legacy are the hacking mechanics. Admittedly, having the power to manipulate an entire city infrastructure with a smartphone is a very new concept that takes some getting used to. It forces you to completely rewire your open-world brain—instead of just shooting your way out of a problem, you learn to trigger massive traffic pile-ups, explode underground steam pipes, and jump between security cameras to ghost through heavily guarded enemy bases. Once the system finally clicks, it is an incredibly satisfying, unique power fantasy. Despite its rough edges, this is a foundational game that still holds up beautifully today.

8/10 — I am a digital god who can black out a city, but I still can't parallel park this minivan.

Watch_Dogs™

A good and enjoyable open-world game with a unique hacking system. Using technology to control traffic lights, cameras, and other devices makes the gameplay feel different from typical action games.

The story is interesting and follows Aiden Pearce’s revenge journey. The darker atmosphere and serious tone give the game its own identity compared to other Ubisoft open-world games.

The graphics are still decent, and Chicago feels alive while exploring the city. Some driving mechanics can feel a bit awkward, and certain missions may become repetitive, but the overall gameplay is still fun.

Overall: A solid open-world game with unique hacking gameplay and a good story.

Rating: 8/10

Watch Dogs has a really interesting concept and feels very different from most other open-world games. The main character, Aiden Pearce, is very well written — he’s subtle, mysterious, and genuinely intriguing throughout the story. The main plot is also interesting, with several twists and turns, although some parts felt predictable to me personally. 
The biggest downside of the game is the side content. After around 10 hours, the side missions slowly start becoming repetitive and boring. The gameplay mechanics can also feel a bit glitchy at times, especially movement, which often feels choppy instead of smooth and fluid. 
One thing I genuinely loved was the driving while listening to the in-game radio. The soundtrack selection is surprisingly entertaining and adds a lot to the atmosphere of the game. 
Overall, I’d give Watch Dogs a 7/10. It’s definitely worth trying, especially if you can grab it during a sale or offer.

An absolutely incredible game!
A wonderful story, definitely worth playing.
This could have been the start of a phenomenal franchise, but unfortunately, for me, the remaining games in the franchise have only declined over time. Watch Dogs 2 isn't bad, but it wasn't what I expected or wanted to see after this game. As for Watch Dogs Legion, it's a completely forgettable game. It has some interesting mechanics, but without a story or a memorable character, the game became just another one among many.
 It would be so good to have a continuation of Aiden Pearce's story. To find out what his life became, if he abandoned crime, if he reunited with his family again, or even if something new would appear in his life to shake things once again.
Truly a game full of potential, completely wasted. I hope that one day Ubisoft will look back, realize the potential of this game, and choose to continue this story, and not let the franchise fall into oblivion.
Still, it's a game I highly recommend everyone to play.

i had the urge to re-play watch dogs 1 for the first time since launch, and boy do i appreciate it a lot more now than i did back then.

at launch in 2014 all i really saw was a bunch of tonal contradictions. this hyper-serious tortured spy/murder man who can "get to anybody, anywhere" but will hack road signs to say things like "gonads and strife weeeee" and "all your base," a game about being a hacker but *not* a part of the big anonymous-style hacker group, the car driving was a mixed bag (you could never know what object would break when you rammed through it and which were invincible momentum killers), etc. now i see a previous iteration of ubisoft titles that, yes, still has open-world bloat, but also had a Lot of presentational details that i think are lost in the assassin's creed origins-era ubisoft, including watch dogs legion. (for the record: i think the last good assassin's creed game was Syndicate. i still go back and play it every few years and it holds up surprisingly well)

character movement feels weighty and connected with the ground, cutscenes are actual motion and facial-captured rather than just preset two shots with canned animations for all dialogue. scenes are directed and performed by actual actors, together in a space, providing appropriate senses of timing and tension that are completely absent in recent ubisoft titles.

watch dogs 1 was always too dark tonally, and it can be especially hard to stomach now that a lot of the horrible underworld practices in the game are basically confirmed as things billionaires and people in power literally do for fun on the regular with no consequence. however, its open world version of chicago is glorious to the point that i would kill for a "black flag resynced"-style remake of the game. constant backstreets and alleys make driving missions tons of fun, even evading cops/gangs which i usually dislike in open-world city games. night time is actually dark rather than just Blue Hours like in every other game, forcing you to rely on city lights, many of which can be broken by driving into them. the blackout ability is, accordingly, so satisfying to use every time.

mechanically, i would take watch dogs 1 over legion any day of the week. legion straight up removed iconic mechanics like being able to time hacks to take out pursuing drivers, and having to chain through cameras (you can just always see any camera now through walls so the level designers dont have to come up with clever architecture to let you chain cameras together). further, aiden's DLC appearance in legion is the only character in that game allowed to use the blackout hack. every character has different things they can do, but as a necessity they are each far less rounded than aiden or marcus from the first two games, making me think/feel less like a cool adaptable hacker and more like "oh shoot my abilities arent the best for this mission, better back out and swap to the one i have that's good for everything. which will incidentally take 5 minutes because of all the menuing and load times":

watch dogs 1's fast travel feels surprisingly seamless compared to modern ubisoft games. there's no lag time when you press the map key while you wait for it to load, and the map zooms in to where you fast traveled without changing to a loading screen. legion, however, makes you wait for the menu to load, wait while you watch the party member you're switching to do their little "you picked me" animation, wait for the load screen, then wait for their little idle animation to play as bagley says "you're the player now" before you can actually play. switching party members should have felt so seamless and quick, like one just runs up behind the current character and tags them out, and you are now just playing that person. assassin's creed shadows had the exact same issue: it is so clunky to switch characters that i only ever played one unless i Had to switch.

finally, characters: aiden is your stock-standard "lone wolf tortured guy" white man video game protagonist, and marcus blows him out of the water as a person i want to play as, but both aiden and marcus actually have Traits to learn about, unlike any of the procgen characters in legion. in legion's base form, the only two characters are the bagley robot and the sabine lady, and every other recruitable character just feels like they brought in horrible forza horizon voice actors to say things about how jolly good this mission will be for dedsec, guv. oi i can't believe they spyin on us normal down-to-earth citizens, the wankers. (frankly, legion's season pass is worth it solely because they allow you to play as aiden and wrench for the vast majority of the base game if you want, so you can actually play as characters with actual character.)

you can see where watch dogs 1 has aged technically, the level of detail distance and texture streaming is pretty bad, but this game has things that make me really miss this era of ubisoft games, at least compared to the current floaty canned-animation mess that is all the recent assassin's creed games.
Story is kinda meh, and Aiden's internal dialogue gets annoying quickly. Gameplay has not aged well, and the gunplay/driving is aids.
